attributes.stem:
An 81-year-old woman with known diabetes and chronic kidney disease is admitted for a hypertensive crisis. On the third day of her hospital stay, though her blood pressure is well controlled, she complains of right knee pain. There is no history of any trauma and she is on metformin, frusemide and perindopril. On examination, her weight is 74 kg, height 164 cm, her blood pressure is 128/80 mmHg, pulse 90/min and temperature 37.2oC. On examination, her right knee shows tenderness, erythema, and swelling, but has full range of motion. Aspiration of synovial fluid shows needle-shaped crystals, and no features of septic joint.

Her serum creatinine is 212 µmol/L (53 - 97), urea 12.5 mmol/L (2.1 - 8.5), serum uric acid 0.51 mmol/L (<0.36 mmol/L) and random blood sugar 12.1 mmol/L (3.2 - 5.5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This case demonstrates an acute attack of monoarticular gout in an elderly person with comorbidities of diabetes and chronic kidney disease. Acute joint pain, swelling, and erythema in a patient with hyperuricaemia and chronic kidney disease is suggestive of gout, though septic joint must be ruled out if there is any suspicion. Aspiration of the affected joint in gout will show the presence of urate crystals which are negatively birefringent under polarised red light, and appear as needle-shaped crystals in the synovial aspirate.

In the above case, the most suitable treatment option is intra-articular corticosteroids. There is a monoarticular presentation of gout, the knee joint is amenable to injection, and the other options mentioned are best avoided in the context of co-morbid diabetes and chronic kidney disease. The patient should be warned to monitor her blood glucose levels for two weeks post injection as the steroid can precipitate higher blood glucose levels.

Systemic corticosteroids can also raise blood sugar levels and long-term use can exacerbate hypertension. In this patient with monoarticular gout and recent hypertensive crisis, an intra-articular corticosteroid may be a better treatment option.

IV or oral NSAIDS like indomethacin or ketorolac can be nephrotoxic and should be avoided where possible in patients with renal disease. In those who are already on an ACE-inhibitor and diuretic as in this case, NSAIDs can also contribute to "triple whammy" complications.

Colchicine is renally cleared and requires a reduced dosage for patients with renal dysfunction. It should not be prescribed for those with advanced kidney disease.
learningPoints: Treatment options for acute gout flares include oral NSAIDs, prednisolone, colchicine and intra-articular corticosteroids
In cases of monoarticular gout, intra-articular corticosteroids can be an effective treatment option
references: Groff GD, Franck WA, Raddatz DA. Systemic steroid therapy for acute gout: a clinical trial and review of the literature. Semin Arthritis Rheum 1990; 19:329-336

Tamez-Pérez HE, Quintanilla-Flores DL, Rodríguez-Gutiérrez R, González-González JG, Tamez-Peña AL. Steroid hyperglycemia: prevalence, early detection and therapeutic recommendations: a narrative review. World J Diabetes. 2015;6(8):1073-1081. doi:10.4239/wjd.v6.i8.1073 [PubMed 26240704]
Solak Y, Atalay H, Biyik Z, et al. Colchicine toxicity in end-stage renal disease patients: a case-control study. Am J Ther. 2014;21(6):e189-e195. doi:10.1097/MJT.0b013e31825a364a [PubMed 22874645]

Wallace SL, Singer JZ, Duncan GJ, Wigley FM, Kuncl RW. Renal function predicts colchicine toxicity: guidelines for the prophylactic use of colchicine in gout. J Rheumatol. 1991;18(2):264-269. [PubMed 2023222]

Stevens PE, Levin A., Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes Chronic Kidney Disease Guideline Development Work Group Members. Evaluation and management of chronic kidney disease: synopsis of the kidney disease: improving global outcomes 2012 clinical practice guideline. Ann Intern Med. 2013 Jun 04;158(11):825-30. [PubMed]

Ingrasciotta Y, Sultana J, Giorgianni F, Fontana A, Santangelo A, Tari DU, Santoro D, Arcoraci V, Perrotta M, Ibanez L, Trifirò G. Association of individual non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and chronic kidney disease: a population-based case control study. PLoS One. 2015;10(4):e0122899. [PMC free article] [PubMed]

Zhang W, Doherty M, Bardin T, Pascual E, Barskova V, Conaghan P, Gerster J, Jacobs J, Leeb B, Lioté F, McCarthy G, Netter P, Nuki G, Perez-Ruiz F, Pignone A, Pimentão J, Punzi L, Roddy E, Uhlig T, Zimmermann-Gòrska I., EULAR Standing Committee for International Clinical Studies Including Therapeutics. EULAR evidence based recommendations for gout. Part II: Management. Report of a task force of the EULAR Standing Committee for International Clinical Studies Including Therapeutics (ESCISIT). Ann Rheum Dis. 2006 Oct;65(10):1312-24. [PMC free article] [PubMed]
